Slate a new pace under ₣BB.

  ## Context

  Single-case access to fixtures is fragmented today. The imprint-channel
  pattern `tt/rbtd-s.FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh [case]` exists only for
  crucible fixtures (tadmor, srjcl, pluml, moriah) — regime-validation,
  enrollment-validation, four-mode, onboarding-sequence, batch-vouch,
  canonical-establish, pristine-lifecycle, access-probe, handbook-render
  have no single-case tabtarget at all. When a fast-suite run reports one
  failed case, there's no surgical re-run; you re-run the whole fixture.

  ## Proposed silks

  single-case-imprint-collapse  (or rename during slate if you see better)

  ## Proposed docket

  # Character

  Tabtarget-surface refactor. Mechanical except for one judgment call:
  the colophon name and channel.

  # Goals

  Replace the imprint-channel `rbtd-s.FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh [case]`
  pattern with a single non-imprint, two-param colophon: param1 is the
  fixture name, param2 is the rust test case function name (e.g.
  `rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels`). The new tabtarget covers ALL
  manifest-registered fixtures, not just crucible ones.

  # Wrap criterion

  - Single new tabtarget routes by fixture-name lookup against the
    rbtd manifest; rejects unknown fixture names
  - Per-fixture FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh files retired
  - Crucible-fixture single-case semantics preserved: still operates on
    already-charged state, never charges/quenches
  - BUK channel discipline honored — the new colophon enrolls under the
    param1 channel, not imprint; zipper updated; CLAUDE.md tabtarget table
    reflects the change
  - Fast suite green; canonical onboarding-sequence cases unchanged

  # Out of scope

  - FixtureRun (whole-fixture) tabtargets — unchanged
  - Charge/quench tabtargets — unchanged
  - Adding new fixtures or new manifest fields

  # References

  - Tools/buk/buk-claude-context.md — channel semantics (imprint vs param1)
  - BBAAz onboarding-sequence-buildout pattern — precedent for
    manifest-driven dispatch
  - rbtd manifest: rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs

  Slate this after ₢BBAAz (rbrv-anchor-locator-format), ordering to
  operator preference within the heat.

Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Feature atop the locator-shape refactor. Lets a vessel's base image
  be an internal hallmark (e.g., airgap's forge-base) rather than only
  an enshrined upstream.
  
  ## Goals
  
  Define how `RBRV_IMAGE_n_ANCHOR` gets populated when the base is a
  hallmark (`hallmarks/<H>/<sigil>:<H>`). Pick the population mechanism:
  
  - (a) enshrine grows internal-sigil resolution → copies hallmark into
    `enshrines/`; ANCHOR points at `enshrines/`
  - (b) separate bridge operation → copies hallmark into `enshrines/`;
    ANCHOR points at `enshrines/`
  - (c) orchestration writes ANCHOR=`hallmarks/<H>/<sigil>:<H>`
    directly → no copy; ANCHOR points at hallmark namespace
  
  ₢BBAAz's locator shape makes (c) most natural — the generic locator
  already supports cross-namespace references. (a) and (b) require a
  physical copy. Decision is mount-time; the asymmetry is real.
  
  Spec the chosen mechanism in RBSAE/RBS0; update enshrine to handle
  already-populated ANCHOR slots (skip-if-set semantics: HEAD-validate
  GAR-resident instead of pulling).
  
  ## Wrap criterion
  
  - Spec defines the hallmark-base population mechanism.
  - Enshrine semantics for already-populated ANCHOR slots are spec'd
    and coded.
  - `rbhoda_director_airgap` handbook prose matches actual operator UX.
  - Onboarding-sequence airgap orchestration is wired and dispatch-able
    (empirical green is ₢BBAAs's wrap, not this one).
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - ₢BBAAz locator-shape refactor — required.
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Empirical onboarding-sequence green — ₢BBAAs verifies.

  ## Character
  
  Feature atop the locator-shape refactor. Lets a vessel's base image
  be an internal hallmark (e.g., airgap's forge-base) rather than only
  an enshrined upstream.
  
  ## Goals
  
  Define how `RBRV_IMAGE_n_ANCHOR` gets populated when the base is a
  hallmark (`hallmarks/<H>/<sigil>:<H>`). Pick the population mechanism:
  
  - (a) enshrine grows internal-sigil resolution → copies hallmark into
    `enshrines/`; ANCHOR points at `enshrines/`
  - (b) separate bridge operation → copies hallmark into `enshrines/`;
    ANCHOR points at `enshrines/`
  - (c) orchestration writes ANCHOR=`hallmarks/<H>/<sigil>:<H>`
    directly → no copy; ANCHOR points at hallmark namespace
  
  ₢BBAAz's locator shape makes (c) most natural — the generic locator
  already supports cross-namespace references. (a) and (b) require a
  physical copy. Decision is mount-time; the asymmetry is real.
  
  Spec the chosen mechanism in RBSAE/RBS0; update enshrine to handle
  already-populated ANCHOR slots (skip-if-set semantics: HEAD-validate
  GAR-resident instead of pulling).
  
  ## Wrap criterion
  
  - Spec defines the hallmark-base population mechanism.
  - Enshrine semantics for already-populated ANCHOR slots are spec'd
    and coded.
  - `rbhoda_director_airgap` handbook prose matches actual operator UX.
  - Onboarding-sequence airgap orchestration is wired and dispatch-able
    (empirical green is ₢BBAAs's wrap, not this one).
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - ₢BBAAz locator-shape refactor — required.
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Empirical onboarding-sequence green — ₢BBAAs verifies.

# jjezs_slate single-case-imprint-collapse

## Character

Tabtarget-surface refactor. Mechanical except for one judgment call:
the colophon name and channel.

## Goals

Replace the imprint-channel `rbtd-s.FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh [case]`
pattern with a single non-imprint, two-param colophon: param1 is the
fixture name, param2 is the rust test case function name (e.g.
`rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels`). The new tabtarget covers ALL
manifest-registered fixtures, not just crucible ones.

## Wrap criterion

- Single new tabtarget routes by fixture-name lookup against the
  rbtd manifest; rejects unknown fixture names

lastPrompt: Here's another thing that is bugging me: with BB we have really leaned into 'enshrines', 'hallmarks', and I think 'reliquaries' as top level GAR subdirectories.  I'm really thinking those need to have…

content.text:
Here's the audit:

**One-letter slots (rbX_) — preferred but mostly blocked**

| Letter | Status |
|---|---|
| `rbe_` (enshrines) | File-prefix free, but `rbev-*` already names vessel directories (`rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge`, etc.). Letter is overloaded — mnemonic clash. |
| `rbh_` (hallmarks) | **TAKEN** — Handbook (non-terminal: rbho, rbhp, rbhw children) |
| `rbr_` (reliquaries) | **TAKEN** — Regime (non-terminal: rbra, rbrn, rbrr, rbrv, rbrm, rbro, rbrp, rbrs) |

So one-letter only barely works for enshrines, and not at all for hallmarks/reliquaries. Asymmetric mintings would itself be confusing.

**Truly-unused first letters (rb tree):** a, b, i, m, n, p, x

**Two-letter family candidates (all free, picked the discriminator letters e/h/r):**

| Family | Enshrines | Hallmarks | Reliquaries | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `rbi*` | `rbie_` | `rbih_` | `rbir_` | These three ARE image namespaces; `rbw-i*` colophons already group them (rbw-ie, rbw-ih, rbw-ir). Strongest semantic fit. |
| `rbg*` | `rbge_` | `rbgh_` | `rbgr_` | They live in Google Artifact Registry; sits under the existing google-machinery family. Slots all open. |
| `rbn*` | `rbne_` | `rbnh_` | `rbnr_` | "n" for namespace; clean slate, weak mnemonic. |

**My recommendation:** the **rbi family**. The three concepts already cluster under `rbw-i*` tabtargets (`rbw-iah`, `rbw-iar`, `rbw-iae`, `rbw-iwh/iwr/iwe`, `rbw-iJh/iJr/iJe`) — `rbi` as image is established in the tabtarget universe but unused as a file prefix, so the primary-universe mintings inherit the existing semantic. Two letters preserves the natural e/h/r discriminator.

Want me to slate this as a separate pace under ₣BB?

message.content: I'm musing therefore 'rbi_es' for enshrines replacement, 'rbi_hm' for hallmark replacement, 'rbi_rq' for reliquaries.  Thoughts?  the -i pattern in tabtargets is technically non-conflicting, right?

content.text:
Two things to separate:

**1. The `rbi_es` shape itself** — that violates the established `prefixXY_word` uniform shape. The discipline (CLAUDE.md "Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline") says concatenated letters then underscore-as-terminator. Existing 5-char prefixes follow this: `rbtdrb_`, `rbtdrc_`, `rbgje*_`, `rbhocc_`. None embed an underscore mid-prefix.

The shape you're after — `rbi` parent + `es/hm/rq` discriminator — is fine; just write it concatenated:

- `rbies_` (enshrines)
- `rbihm_` (hallmarks)
- `rbirq_` (reliquaries)

That's a 2-letter discriminator after a 1-letter parent — well within the hard 2-letter ceiling.

**2. The tabtarget `-i` collision question** — yes, technically non-conflicting. Tabtarget colophons (`rbw-i*` family) live in the colophon universe; primary-universe prefixes (`rbi*_`) are an orthogonal namespace. No tool collides on lookup. And the semantic alignment is actually a *win*: `rbw-iaH.AuditHallmark.sh` would naturally route through `rbihm_audit_*()` in code.

**The real choice — 1-letter vs 2-letter discriminator:**

| Form | Pro | Con |
|---|---|---|
| `rbie_` / `rbih_` / `rbir_` | Mirrors existing tabtarget discriminators exactly (`rbw-i**a**h`, `rbw-i**a**r`, `rbw-i**a**e`); tightest possible | Single-letter requires the e/h/r convention to be carried in your head |
| `rbies_` / `rbihm_` / `rbirq_` | Self-documenting at the prefix; "hm" reads obviously as hallmark | One letter further from the tabtarget colophon convention; minor `hm` overlap with HMK kit prefix (different universe, but a reader could pause) |

I'd lean `rbie_/rbih_/rbir_` for tightness with the tabtarget family — but `rbies_/rbihm_/rbirq_` is defensible if you value the readability and accept the slightly weaker mirroring. Either way, drop the embedded underscore.

message.content: I do prefer my original in this place due to the premium on extra characters.  We need this unique, not long and descriptive.  The kindle constant or tinder constant name that holds this short value should indeed be long and descriptive.  Is that clear?

content.text:
Yes, clear — and I had the wrong frame on the previous turn. You're talking about **GAR path-component literals**, not code-identifier prefixes:

- value (short, unique): `rbi_es`, `rbi_hm`, `rbi_rq` — what actually appears in `gar.../<project>/rbi_es/<vessel>:<tag>`
- constant name (long, self-explaining): something like `RBI_ENSHRINES_GAR_NAMESPACE` / `RBI_HALLMARKS_GAR_NAMESPACE` / `RBI_RELIQUARIES_GAR_NAMESPACE`

That makes the prefix-discipline rules I was citing irrelevant — `rbi_es` isn't a code-identifier prefix, so the "no embedded underscore in `prefixXY_`" concern doesn't bind. It's a path token.

For the path token itself, three properties matter:
- **Unique within GAR namespace siblings** ✓ — `es`/`hm`/`rq` are mutually distinct and don't collide with anything else under the project root
- **Stable** ✓ — these are wire-format values, baked into provenance, vouches, locator strings
- **Readable enough at a glance** ✓ — `rbi_` anchor signals "recipemuster image namespace", suffix discriminates the three categories

The kindle/tinder constant names carry the meaning at the call site; the wire value just has to be unique and stable.

Ready to slate this as a pace under ₣BB? It would land alongside ₢BBAA1 (single-case-imprint-collapse) but is independent — the locator-shape work in ₢BBAAz is the prerequisite for substituting these path tokens cleanly.

message.content: yes.  slate the pace now.  Then make a guess as to how many files need to be updated to emplace this fix.  That guess will inform whether we handle this pace sooner or later.

# jjezs_slate gar-namespace-path-tokens

## Character

Wire-format value rename. Mechanical sweep across all GAR path
producers and consumers; no semantic change.

## Goals

Replace the three GAR top-level subdirectory literals with short,
unique, anchored path tokens:

- `enshrines/`   → `rbi_es/`
- `hallmarks/`   → `rbi_hm/`
- `reliquaries/` → `rbi_rq/`

Hold each value in a named constant (kindle or tinder, per call-site
convention) — long descriptive name, short value:

- `RBI_ENSHRINES_GAR_NAMESPACE="rbi_es"`
- `RBI_HALLMARKS_GAR_NAMESPACE="rbi_hm"`
- `RBI_RELIQUARIES_GAR_NAMESPACE="rbi_rq"`

The `rbi_` lead anchors the token as recipemuster-image-namespace;
the suffix discriminates the three categories. Tabtarget colophons
(`rbw-i*`) and prefix discipline are unchanged — this is value-space
only.

## Wrap criterion

- Three named constants emplaced in the appropriate kindle/tinder
  module.
- All call sites composing GAR locators reference the constants
  rather than the bare literal strings.
- Locator parses (the inline `${anchor%:*}` / `${anchor##*:}` pattern
  used at three sites in BBAAz) operate identically on the new tokens.
- Existing populated values in vessel rbrv.env files migrated
  (the one ANCHOR field repo-wide carrying `enshrines/...`).
- Spec sweep: RBS0/RBSAE/RBSAC/RBSAG and any other doc that names the
  GAR namespace literals reflects the new tokens.
- Handbook prose using these literals as examples updated.
- Fast suite green; canonical onboarding-sequence cases unchanged.

## Out of scope

- Code-identifier prefix changes (`rbie_`, `rbih_`, `rbir_` minting
  was considered and rejected — wire-format only).
- New tabtarget colophons; the existing `rbw-i*` family is unchanged.
- Migration of any abandoned-vessel registry artifacts under the old
  paths — fresh-build assumption.

## References

- BBAAz locator-shape refactor — locator parsing pattern; this pace
  swaps the values flowing through that pattern.
- Tools/buk/buk-claude-context.md — kindle/tinder convention.

content: I wonder if we ought to have a quoin for each of reliquary/hallmark/enshrine section of GAR?

content.text:
Good — the architecture is already there. The spec corpus is **one document** assembled by `include::` from RBS0, and RBS0's mapping section is where linked-term attributes live (`:rbtr_role:`, `:at_operator:`, etc. — all visible across every included spec).

So the sprue mechanism is RBS0's mapping section itself. Three quoins land there:

```adoc
:gar_enshrines_namespace:   <<gar_enshrines_namespace,rbi_es>>
:gar_hallmarks_namespace:   <<gar_hallmarks_namespace,rbi_hm>>
:gar_reliquaries_namespace: <<gar_reliquaries_namespace,rbi_rq>>
```

with definition sites carrying the literal as display text + a one-line definition. Then spec prose changes from `enshrines/<anchor>` to `{gar_enshrines_namespace}/<anchor>`, which renders as `rbi_es/<anchor>` and links back to the definition. Future rename = one line.

**Implications for BBAA2:**

- File count is similar (~19 specs still get touched once for the literal→reference swap), but the *value* of touching them goes up: each spec gains a click-through to the canonical definition rather than carrying a stale literal.
- RBS0 gains three quoins; the literal lives in exactly one place (the definition site's display text).
- Bash/Rust code still uses kindle/tinder constants — same as before, just now with a peer in the spec corpus.
- One subtle gotcha: AsciiDoc attribute substitution behavior in listing blocks (`----`) — if any code samples in spec prose include the literal, those blocks need `subs="attributes"` or a sibling literal value. Need to audit during the sweep.

**Recommendation:** reslate BBAA2 to make the sprue mechanism explicit in the docket — it changes the implementation strategy from "find/replace 25 files" to "mint three quoins in RBS0, then swap literals to references across the spec corpus and emplace constants in code." Same number of files touched, very different artifact produced.

Want me to reslate?

message.content: I wonder if we ought to have a quoin for each of reliquary/hallmark/enshrine section of GAR?

content.text:
Yes — and the distinction earns its keep:

**Two quoins per concept, not one.** The wire-format token and the conceptual region serve different references and need different display text:

| Quoin | Type | Display | Used for |
|---|---|---|---|
| `gar_enshrines_section` | concept | "Enshrines" | "...files held in the {gar_enshrines_section}", "...the {gar_enshrines_section} contains copies of upstream base images" |
| `gar_enshrines_namespace` | value | "rbi_es" | "...path {gar_enshrines_namespace}/<anchor>", "...locator format {gar_enshrines_namespace}/<a>:<a>" |
| `gar_hallmarks_section` | concept | "Hallmarks" | conceptual prose |
| `gar_hallmarks_namespace` | value | "rbi_hm" | path strings |
| `gar_reliquaries_section` | concept | "Reliquaries" | conceptual prose |
| `gar_reliquaries_namespace` | value | "rbi_rq" | path strings |

Six quoins. The section quoin's definition body explains what lives there and links to its namespace quoin; the namespace quoin's definition body just declares the wire value and links back.

**Why not one quoin per concept?** If `{gar_enshrines}` rendered as `rbi_es`, prose like "the enshrines hold copies" becomes "the rbi_es hold copies" — wrong category. The section concept and the wire token are read in different contexts and need different display text. That's load-bearing distinction; collapsing them creates worse prose.

**Why this matters for BBAA2:** the sweep across the 19 specs needs to triage each occurrence — is this prose referring to the *region* or quoting the *path token*? The section quoins make the spec corpus cleaner *and* surface the distinction during the sweep, so each replacement has to pick the right one. That's a small audit-quality dividend on top of the central-value benefit.

**Hierarchy hook:** these three section quoins also become the natural anchor points for "what lives where" prose anywhere in the corpus — `rbst_locator` (the form) sits upstream; the three section quoins are siblings beneath it; the three namespace quoins are values bound to each section. Tidy.

Want me to reslate BBAA2 with the six-quoin scope?

content.text:
Yeah, you're right — and the load-bearing test confirms it.

Reading the source, `{gar_enshrines_namespace}` is unmistakably a cross-reference. Reading the render, `rbi_es` appears in path contexts where the literal *belongs* — `{gar_enshrines_namespace}/<anchor>` renders as `rbi_es/<anchor>` and reads correctly. The awkward sentence I conjured earlier ("the rbi_es hold copies") is a strawman: that prose would just be written as plain English ("the enshrines hold copies") with no quoin at all, because the conceptual reference doesn't need anchoring.

The clean rule:

- **Quoin** when quoting the wire-format path token → `{gar_enshrines_namespace}/<anchor>`
- **Bare English** when referring to the conceptual region → "files held in the enshrines"

The section concept doesn't earn a quoin because its conceptual anchor already exists implicitly via RBSAE (ark_enshrine procedural spec), RBSAG (graft), etc. — those are the canonical definition sites for what populates each region. Adding section quoins would duplicate without paying back.

So: **3 quoins, scope confirmed**. `gar_enshrines_namespace`, `gar_hallmarks_namespace`, `gar_reliquaries_namespace`.

Agreed — I was using "sprue" loosely; the actual mechanism is an AsciiDoc attribute reference with a literal value (`:gar_enshrines_namespace: rbi_es`), not a linked-term quoin with a definition site. Lighter, simpler, and exactly the right tool for a wire-format value where there's nothing to "click through to" — the value is the value. The descriptive name lives in the attribute key; the constants in code carry the same name with the same value.
